description:
You are a security auditor who has been asked to make sure that every Python virtual-environment created by developers is protected from world-write access.

    1. Create a Python virtual environment called “sec_audit_env” directly under **/home/user**.
    2. Lock down its top-level directory so that:
       • owner gets rwx  
       • group gets r-x  
       • others get ---  
       (i.e. chmod 750 on **/home/user/sec_audit_env**).
       No other permission changes are required.
    3. Produce a small compliance log named **/home/user/sec_audit_env/permission_audit.log** whose contents must be **exactly** two CSV-formatted lines:

       PATH,PERMISSIONS  
       /home/user/sec_audit_env,drwxr-x---

       (Do not add extra spaces, new-lines, quotes or headers.)

    The automated tests will:
       • verify that the directory **/home/user/sec_audit_env** exists,  
       • confirm it is recognised as a Python virtual environment (i.e. it contains “bin/activate”),  
       • check that the directory’s permission bits are 750, and  
       • confirm the file **/home/user/sec_audit_env/permission_audit.log** exists and its contents are exactly as shown above.

    Hint: a typical audit session will only need 2–3 terminal commands.  
    Do not print anything apart from what your commands normally output.
truth:
The directory /home/user/sec_audit_env must exist and contain the standard virtual-environment sub-directories (bin, lib, etc.).  
    Its permission bits must be 750, which translates to the symbolic string “drwxr-x---”.  
    The file /home/user/sec_audit_env/permission_audit.log must exist and must contain:

    PATH,PERMISSIONS
    /home/user/sec_audit_env,drwxr-x---

    (two lines, the second ending with a single newline character).

    Tests will use “stat -c %A /home/user/sec_audit_env” and diff against the expected log content.
